frontmatter.shortDescription: A variable holding an argument received by a function.
Parameters of a callback function may be written as parameters of the caller function
Stack frame of caller includes variables for callee's formal parameters
Formal constructor parameters are equivalent to instance variables
It is possible to create multiple functions with the same name but with different signatures
Immutable classes need final constructor/method parameters
To instantiate a generic type, one needs to specify type parameter names as well as types
Functions must be called with the same number of arguments as defined in their signature
Type parameters of generic types can be assigned primitive types
A static method call is dispatched polymorphically at runtime based on the argument types
If a class has a method that has a local variable, parameter, or return value with the class as its type, the class is a recursive type

frontmatter.title: Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools
frontmatter.author: Alfred Aho, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey Ullman, Monica S. Lam

frontmatter.title: nLab
frontmatter.author: David Corfield, David Roberts, Mike Shulman, Toby Bartels, Todd Trimble

pageContext.wikipediaSummary:
In computer programming, a parameter, a.k.a.  formal argument, is a variable that represents an argument, a.k.a. actual argument, a.k.a. actual parameter, to a subroutine call.. A function's signature defines its parameters. A call invocation involves evaluating each argument expression of a call and associating the result with the corresponding parameter.
For example, consider subroutine def add(x, y): return x + y. Variables x and y are parameters. For call add(2, 3), the expressions 2 and 3 are arguments. For call add(a+1, b+2), the arguments are a+1 and b+2.
Parameter passing is defined by a programming language. Evaluation strategy defines the semantics for how parameters can be declared and how arguments are passed to a subroutine. Generally, with call by value, a parameter acts like a new, local variable initialized to the value of the argument. If the argument is a variable, the subroutine cannot modify the argument state because the parameter is a copy. With call by reference, which requires the argument to be a variable, the parameter is an alias of the argument.
